fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to ship a small logging utility — think of it like the routing layer we built for the notification service, but for log output instead. The core idea is that app code should just call one thing and not care where logs end up. We want to support multiple 'destinations' at once, so if someone wants logs going to the console AND some remote tracker simultaneously, that just works without touching the call sites.

We also got feedback from the on-call team that when things go wrong at 2am, it's really hard to visually scan a terminal and spot the critical stuff. So the console destination should make scary stuff visually pop — warnings and crashes should look obviously different from normal info messages. The exact color scheme follows what the design team agreed on internally (check the last design sync notes).

One more thing from the security folks: sometimes you want to log something but specifically NOT send it to certain destinations — like you might not want to fire off a crash report to the external vendor for every little warning. That selective suppression needs to work per-call without permanently removing any destination from the list.

The whole thing needs a CLI test harness that can run JSON-driven scenarios against it. Keep infra concerns out of the business logic please — same pattern we used on the auth refactor.

One quick follow-up from the questions that came in: there are exactly four severity levels here, and they should stay tight and predictable: 'message', 'warning', 'fatal', and 'error'. The first three take a plain text message string as the payload. 'error' is the odd one out and should take an exception value, with an optional stacktrace if that’s available, rather than a normal message string. Also, every log call should hand back the original payload unchanged so callers can keep chaining things naturally — for message/warning/fatal that means the same message string comes back, and for error it means the same exception value comes back.

On the shape of the implementation, each sink needs to understand all four severity levels: informational (message), warning, fatal, and error. Keep that interface small and cohesive so each method maps cleanly to one severity level. The main src/ pattern should be the broadcasting facade approach — specifically the LogFacade or equivalent class that holds a list of registered sinks and fans out each log call to all of them, returning the original payload. The important design bit is that new sinks can be added without modifying the facade itself, and the facade should depend on the sink abstraction rather than any concrete sink.

Also confirming the per-call suppression behavior: each log call can take an optional exclusion list named 'exceptFor' in the JSON contract. That list names sink kinds to skip just for that one call. If a registered sink’s kind matches something in 'exceptFor', it gets nothing for that call. Everything else still receives the log normally, and the original logged value is still returned. Nothing is removed or permanently changed there — the sink is NOT unregistered, and it should continue receiving other calls as usual.

And for the console formatting details, warning and fatal need to match the exact terminal treatment. Warning uses xterm 256-color index 0 as foreground (black) and index 3 as background (yellow). The activation sequences are ESC[38;5;0m for foreground and ESC[48;5;3m for background, where ESC is byte 0x1B, and the rendered text is closed with ESC[0m. The full line format is: <fg_seq><bg_seq>[WARNING] <message><reset>. Fatal uses xterm 256-color index 7 as foreground (white) and index 1 as background (red). The activation sequences are ESC[38;5;7m for foreground and ESC[48;5;1m for background. The full line format is: <fg_seq><bg_seq>[FATAL] <message><reset>.

trigger_keywords: how is the error payload formatted in the colorizing sink
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Error uses xterm 256-color index 15 as foreground (bold/bright white) and index 88 as background (dark red). The payload after the [ERROR] tag is structured as: { exception: <value>, stacktrace: <value> }. The full line format is: ESC[38;5;15mESC[48;5;88m[ERROR] { exception: <exception>, stacktrace: <stacktrace> }ESC[0m

trigger_keywords: how is the message level rendered by the colorizing sink
plain text rendering for message level
hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
The informational 'message' level is rendered with NO color sequences at all. The output is simply: [MESSAGE] <message>
 — an uppercase tag in square brackets, a single space, the message text, and a newline. No ANSI escape sequences are applied.

trigger_keywords: how does the test runner work
what does the CLI adapter expect as input
JSON input format for the adapter
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The execution adapter reads a single JSON object from stdin. The 'action' field selects behavior: 'log' registers sinks named in 'clients', issues each call in the 'calls' array (each with 'level' and either 'message' or 'exception', plus an optional 'exceptFor' list), and prints a 'return=<payload>' line followed by one 'client=<name> level=<level> message=<msg>' (or 'exception=<val>') line per receiving sink. The 'colorize' action drives the colorizing sink for a single entry and prints the rendered line directly. Color output must be emitted unconditionally, regardless of whether stdout is a TTY.

trigger_keywords: how to run the test suite
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The test harness entry point is 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh'. It accepts an optional '--cases-dir <subdir>' argument (defaulting to 'public_test_cases'). For each test case it writes raw stdout output to 'rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/<filename_stem>@<case_index_zero_padded_3>.txt'. Output is namespaced by cases-dir so different runs never overwrite each other. Each .txt file contains only the raw program stdout — no PASS/FAIL metadata.

trigger_keywords: how should sinks be ordered when broadcasting
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Sinks receive each log call in registration order — the order in which they were registered with the facade. This applies to every severity level and is visible in the output as one line per sink in that same registration order, after the 'return=' line.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

tell me all the action names
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'action' field. Valid values are: 'log', 'colorize'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the color scheme follows what the design team agreed on internally (check the last design sync notes)
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The specific xterm 256-color indices: warning=fg:0/bg:3, fatal=fg:7/bg:1, error=fg:15/bg:88, encoded as ANSI SGR sequences ESC[38;5;<n>m (foreground) and ESC[48;5;<n>m (background) with ESC[0m reset

context_pointers.vague_reference: same pattern we used on the auth refactor
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Strict separation of the core domain (facade + sinks) from the I/O/JSON adapter layer — the execution adapter in the CLI entry point reads JSON from stdin and translates it into method calls on the core system, while the core system has no knowledge of stdin, stdout, or JSON

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect handy — you'd need to dig into the existing codebase or check with the original devs who spec'd this out. Try looking at the test cases in rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ for concrete examples of expected behavior.

feature1_broadcast_and_return.json.description: A logging facade broadcasts every log call to all registered sinks and returns the value that was logged. Each call carries a severity level (informational message, warning, fatal, or error) and a payload (a text message, or for the error level an exception value). When one or more sinks are registered, issuing a call delivers that call to each sink in registration order and yields back the original payload unchanged. The output reports, per call in the order issued, a line with the returned payload, followed by one line for each sink that received the call, naming the sink, the severity level, and the delivered payload.

cases.expected_output:
return=Message
client=A level=message message=Message
return=Warning
client=A level=warning message=Warning
return=Fatal
client=A level=fatal message=Fatal
return=Error
client=A level=error exception=Error

return=Message
client=A level=message message=Message
client=B level=message message=Message

feature2_except_for.json.description: When issuing a log call, specific sinks can be excluded by their kind so the call is not delivered to them, while every other registered sink still receives it and the logged value is still returned. The exclusion list names sink kinds to skip; a sink whose kind is excluded receives nothing at all for that call. The output reports the returned payload followed by one line for each sink that actually received the call; excluded sinks contribute no line. This works the same way across all severity levels.

feature3_1_message.json.description: A colorizing sink renders an informational log entry as a single line with no color treatment. The line consists of an uppercase level tag in square brackets, a single space, and the original message text, terminated by a newline.

feature3_2_warning.json.description: A colorizing sink renders a warning log entry as a single line wrapped in ANSI SGR escape sequences for a black foreground over a yellow background. The line begins with the color-activation sequences, then an uppercase level tag in square brackets, a space, the message text, and finally the reset sequence, terminated by a newline. The foreground is xterm color index 0 and the background is xterm color index 3; each is introduced with the escape byte (0x1B), an opening bracket, the SGR selector, and is closed by the reset sequence (escape byte, opening bracket, zero, lowercase m).

feature3_3_fatal.json.description: A colorizing sink renders a fatal log entry as a single line wrapped in ANSI SGR escape sequences for a white foreground over a red background. The line begins with the color-activation sequences, then an uppercase level tag in square brackets, a space, the message text, and finally the reset sequence, terminated by a newline. The foreground is xterm color index 7 and the background is xterm color index 1.

feature3_4_error.json.description: A colorizing sink renders an error log entry as a single line wrapped in ANSI SGR escape sequences for a bold white foreground over a dark-red background. The payload is composed from an exception value and a stacktrace value laid out as a structured fragment: an uppercase level tag in square brackets, a space, then an opening brace, the labeled exception value, a comma, the labeled stacktrace value, and a closing brace. The whole fragment is wrapped between the color-activation sequences and the reset sequence, terminated by a newline. The foreground is xterm color index 15 and the background is xterm color index 88.

cases.expected_output: [38;5;15m[48;5;88m[ERROR] { exception: Connection refused, stacktrace: at socket.connect }[0m
